# **A Literature Review of Machine Learning World Models: Technical Architectures, Alignment Vulnerabilities, and the Symbolic Ethical Paradigm of SHU-WM**

The engineering of an internal representation of the physical environment, commonly formalized as a world model, represents a major milestone in model-based reinforcement learning and autonomous systems.1 By anticipating how the environment evolves and predicting the consequences of actions before executing them, world models enable sample-efficient, dream-based policy optimization.3 However, as autonomous systems are deployed in increasingly complex, safety-critical domains, the trade-offs between continuous learned dynamics and symbolic, verifiable architectures have become highly salient.5  
This review provides a comprehensive technical analysis of the world model landscape. It begins with the mathematical foundations of learned neural latent-dynamics networks, tracing their evolution through autoregressive and diffusion-based paradigms. It then examines the emergence of neuro-symbolic and code-based world models designed to introduce explicit logic and sample efficiency. Next, it diagnoses the value alignment crisis and the philosophical limits of static value specifications, known as the "specification trap." Finally, it introduces the Symbolic Philosophy-Transcribed World Model (SHU-WM)—a training-free, symbolic simulation engine with a tunable morality dial—and details its empirical validation against standard learned baselines.

## **The Evolution and Foundations of Learned Neural World Models**

The conceptual framework for modern neural world models was established through a modular spatial-temporal architecture composed of three interconnected networks, designated as the Vision-Memory-Controller (V-M-C) triad.4

                       \[ Environment (Pixels) \]  
                                  │  
                                  ▼ (Spatial Compression)  
                            \[ Vision (VAE) \]  
                                  │  
                                  ▼ Latent Vector (z\_t)  
                            ◄─── Action (a\_t)  
                                  │  
                                  ▼ Latent State (h\_t)  
                          \[ Controller (Policy) \]

In this classic configuration, raw sensory observations are compressed into a compact latent representation by a spatial encoder, which is subsequently processed by a recurrent dynamics model to simulate temporal transitions.4

### **Mathematical Foundations of the Vision-Memory-Controller (V-M-C) Triad**

The foundational world model architecture relies on a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to parameterize the spatial vision module (![][image1]), projecting high-dimensional raw observations ![][image2] into a low-dimensional bottleneck representation ![][image3].4 The temporal transition dynamics are governed by the Memory module (![][image4]), historically implemented as a Mixture Density Network paired with a Recurrent Neural Network (MDN-RNN).4 The MDN-RNN maintains a deterministic recurrent hidden state ![][image5] while outputting a probability distribution over the next latent state ![][image6] conditioned on the previous latent state and action:  
![][image7]  
where ![][image8] represent the mixture coefficients, means, and variances of a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM).4  
In modern implementations, GPU acceleration has significantly optimized this training loop.9 Utilizing high-performance JAX-based frameworks and vectorization operations (e.g., jax.vmap), researchers can simulate the recurrent "dreams" of hundreds of parallel agents simultaneously on a single GPU.9 This parallelization transforms evolutionary policy search from a slow, sequential CPU-bound loop into a highly efficient batched operation.9  
To bridge the Sim2Real gap—where agents exploit inaccuracies in the simulated world model—implementations introduce curriculum learning alongside specialized loss formulations.9 For example, a curriculum can be constructed by running an agent, identifying failures (such as slipping on sharp corners in a physics simulation), collecting on-policy failure trajectories, and injecting this critical data back into the training distribution.9 Furthermore, an asymmetric loss function can be deployed to penalize "optimism bias," wherein the dynamics network hallucinates safer outcomes (such as high rewards during a collision) than physical reality dictates.9 By penalizing overestimation of rewards significantly more than underestimation:  
![][image9]  
the world model is forced to maintain conservative, physically grounded dynamics.9

### **Advanced Latent-Dynamics and the Dreamer Family**

To stabilize training across complex tasks without domain-specific hyperparameter tuning, the Dreamer family (V1, V2, and V3) introduced the Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM).10 The RSSM architecture splits the state representation into a deterministic recurrent hidden state ![][image5] and a stochastic latent state ![][image10].10 Unlike early architectures that parameterized ![][image10] as a continuous Gaussian distribution—which often suffered from representation collapse and backpropagation instability—DreamerV2 and V3 shifted to discrete, categorical latent variables.10  
Under this categorical formulation, the latent space is structured as a grid of ![][image4] discrete variables, each having ![][image11] classes (typically a ![][image12] grid of one-hot vectors).10 To propagate gradients through these discrete samples during variational inference, straight-through gradient estimators are utilized.10 The transition dynamics are optimized using a three-part objective containing an observation reconstruction loss, a reward prediction loss, and a KL balancing loss to align the prior and posterior distributions.10 The RSSM transition equations follow a strict dependency chain:  
![][image13]  
![][image14]  
![][image15]  
![][image16]  
The standard KL divergence is modified with "free bits" (clipping the loss below ![][image17] nat) to prevent the prior from collapsing to a trivial representation, thereby forcing the model to focus capacity on predicting environmental features.10 DreamerV3 introduces several additional mechanisms for cross-domain stability:

* **Symlog Transform:** Squashes wide-ranging reward and value distributions using the function ![][image18] to preserve gradient scale.10  
* **Two-Hot Encoding:** Categorical targets are represented as a soft distribution over discrete bins (typically ![][image19] bins) to provide a richer gradient signal than standard scalar regression.10  
* **Unimix:** Blends the categorical probability output with a small uniform distribution (![][image20]) to prevent probability collapse and maintain exploratory capacity during long-term imaginations.14

### **Planning-Centric, Autoregressive, and Diffusion Paradigms**

While the Dreamer family focuses on pixel reconstruction to shape the latent space, alternative paradigms decouple planning from visual generation.15

#### **MuZero and Task-Agnostic Representation**

The MuZero framework optimizes its internal state ![][image21] purely to predict task-relevant quantities: the policy distribution ![][image22], the value function ![][image23], and the immediate scalar reward ![][image24], entirely omitting pixel-level reconstruction.15 Under this formulation, the latent state is not grounded in visual semantics but is trained end-to-end via multi-step unrolling to match search-derived targets.16  
However, MuZero-style architectures suffer from "incomplete context" during inference.17 Because the latent states are tightly coupled to the historical action trajectory, compounding prediction errors over extended unrolling horizons degrade the accuracy of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS).17 To address this, variants like UniZero utilize a modular transformer-based dynamics engine equipped with a Key-Value (KV) cache to maintain a stable historical context, outperforming classical Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) unrolling.17

#### **Autoregressive Token-Based Sequence Models**

A parallel approach treats world modeling as a discrete sequence modeling problem, projecting visual frames into categorical tokens.19 The IRIS agent utilizes a Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to compress images into a sequence of discrete tokens, and applies a generative autoregressive Transformer (GPT-like architecture) to predict subsequent tokens over time.19  
To overcome the heavy computational burden of autoregressive generation over long sequences, ![][image25]\-IRIS introduces a disentangled dynamics engine.21 ![][image25]\-IRIS utilizes a conditional autoencoder that only encodes "stochastic deltas"—the temporal changes between frames that cannot be directly inferred from actions—drastically reducing the token budget and accelerating training by an order of magnitude.21

#### **Continuous Space Diffusion Models**

DIAMOND represents a departure from discrete tokenization by training a reinforcement learning agent directly within a continuous diffusion UNet.3 By modeling the transition probability ![][image26] as a reverse denoising process, DIAMOND preserves high-fidelity visual details and captures multi-modal distributions that are lost during discrete bottleneck compression.3 This continuous approach achieves a mean human-normalized score of 1.46 on the Atari 100k benchmark.3  
At the scale frontier, Google DeepMind's Genie family scales generative interactive environments to 11 billion parameters.22 Trained unsupervised from unlabeled internet videos, Genie generates action-controllable, playable 3D environments in real-time (24 frames per second at 720p resolution), serving as interactive neural game engines and simulation platforms for robotics.1 For autonomous driving, the Waymo World Model (a specialized adaptation of Genie 3 released in February 2026\) simulates extreme driving edge cases—such as sudden weather hazards—converting phone and dashcam footage into interactive, high-fidelity LiDAR simulations at four times the baseline frame rate.23

#### **Decoder-Free and Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures**

To address environments with high-entropy visual noise—such as a background TV screen playing task-irrelevant footage—decoder-free Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA-WMs) bypass pixel reconstruction.15 Frameworks like NE-Dreamer replace reconstruction with next-embedding prediction, utilizing a lightweight causal temporal transformer to align predicted latent states with target representations produced by an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) encoder.16  
Using Barlow Twins or similar redundancy-reduction objectives, JEPA-WMs eliminate spatial detail that does not contribute to temporal predictive alignment, focusing capacity strictly on controllable and task-relevant environmental semantics.16

| Architecture | Latent Dimension / State Representation | Dynamics Engine | Training Objective / Loss Function | Primary Benchmark Performance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Ha & Schmidhuber (2018)** 4 | ![][image27]\-dim continuous vector ![][image10] via VAE.7 | MDN-RNN (GMM \+ LSTM).7 | Reconstruction MSE \+ KL Divergence \+ GMM Negative Log-Likelihood.4 | Solved CarRacing-v3 and Doom VizDoom environments.4 |
| **DreamerV3 (2023)** 10 | ![][image12] discrete categorical variables.13 | Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM with GRU).10 | Reconstruction Symlog MSE \+ Two-Hot CE Reward \+ KL Balancing.10 | First model to collect diamonds in Minecraft from scratch without human data.10 |
| **MuZero (2020)** 17 | Abstract, task-specific ungrounded latent state.18 | Recurrent MLP Transition Network.17 | Policy Cross-Entropy \+ Value TD-Loss \+ Scalar Reward Loss.18 | Superhuman performance in Go, Chess, Shogi, and Atari.20 |
| **IRIS (2023)** 20 | Discrete spatial tokens via VQ-VAE.20 | Autoregressive GPT-style Transformer.20 | VQ commitment loss \+ Next-token Cross-Entropy.20 | Mean Human Normalized Score of 1.046 on Atari 100k.20 |
| **DIAMOND (2024)** 3 | Continuous high-dimensional image space.3 | Diffusion UNet (SDE reverse-denoising).3 | Denoising Score Matching MSE conditioned on action history.3 | Mean Human Normalized Score of 1.46 on Atari 100k.3 |
| **Genie 3 (2025)** 23 | Spatiotemporal video tokens.22 | Autoregressive Dynamics Model \+ Latent Action Model.22 | Frame-prediction Cross-Entropy (unsupervised action discovery).22 | Real-time 720p 3D interactive world generation at 24 fps.23 |
| **NE-Dreamer (2026)** 16 | Decoder-free latent embedding.16 | Causal Temporal Transformer.16 | Barlow Twins Redundancy-Reduction Next-Embedding Alignment.16 | Matches DreamerV3 on DeepMind Control; outperforms on DMLab Rooms.16 |

## **The Neuro-Symbolic Paradigm and Code World Models**

To mitigate the massive data requirements and representational opacity of purely neural world models, researchers have turned to Code World Models (CWMs) and neuro-symbolic architectures.5 By representing environmental dynamics, state transitions, and reward structures as executable code, CWMs combine the logical precision of symbolic programs with the high-level reasoning of deep language models.5

### **Executable Code as an Internal Simulation Engine**

In a typical Code World Model, an agent's internal model is represented as high-level, executable code (predominantly Python), which formally defines state transitions, legal actions, reward calculations, and termination predicates.5 This code-as-model configuration offers three distinct advantages over neural latents:

1. **Verifiability:** The code can be formally analyzed, unit-tested, and edited to correct logical bugs or adjust environmental rules, ensuring predictable execution boundaries.5  
2. **Compute Efficiency:** By decoupling model generation (synthesizing the code once) from model execution (running the synthesized Python functions), CWMs bypass the high computational cost of running neural network inference during multi-step planning rollouts.5  
3. **Out-of-Distribution Generalization:** Programs represent abstract rules that generalize perfectly to novel scales, object configurations, and game levels where neural transition models typically suffer from compounding visual drift.32

In the program synthesis landscape, the WorldCoder framework utilizes an LLM to generate candidate Python world models from sparse trajectory data.30 It organizes the generated code in a hypothesis tree and employs a Thompson-sampling bandit strategy to select and iteratively refine the most promising programs.30  
Similarly, the GIF-MCTS framework structures program synthesis as a Monte Carlo Tree Search over code edits.30 It evaluates candidate models against trajectory datasets and uses execution errors and prediction mismatches as logical feedback for chain-of-thought debugging prompts, automating the correction of complex dynamics code.30  
In multi-agent and imperfect-information environments, synthesizing symbolic specifications is more challenging.31 Lehrach et al. address this by prompting an LLM to generate a joint autoencoder structure: an inference function (the encoder) that maps partial observations to a plausible hidden game state, and a transition function (the decoder) that simulates moves and enforces legal rules.31 This synthesized specification provides a formal framework for Monte Carlo Tree Search, allowing planners to navigate stochastic, partially observable games (such as Poker) without illegal actions.31  
To scale program synthesis to complex domains where single monolithic programs struggle, the PoE-World (Product of programmatic Experts) framework decomposes the world program into hundreds of small, independent programmatic experts.32 Each synthesized expert models an independent physical law or causal transition.32 The individual expert outputs are mathematically aggregated through exponential weights, which are optimized via gradient descent:  
![][image28]  
This formulation allows PoE-World to build accurate, planning-capable world models for complex Atari games (such as Montezuma's Revenge) from less than a minute of visual demonstration data.32

### **High-Level Task Planning via PDDL and Answer Set Programming**

For high-level task planning in robotics and aerial control, architectures translate natural language descriptions or visual trajectories directly into formal symbolic representations, such as the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL).35 A symbolic PDDL domain defines the planning space through entities, boolean or numerical predicates, initial states, and operators with explicit preconditions and effects:  
![][image29]  
Once this domain is formally specified, classical systematic search algorithms (such as ![][image30] or Fast Downward) can be applied to find optimal task plans, avoiding the risk-averse local minima and slow inference speeds of LLM planners.35  
To generate high-quality PDDL domain files without domain-specific training data, researchers scale up the test-time computation of LLMs.36 The instance Verbalized Machine Learning (iVML) framework employs a Best-of-N sampling strategy to generate initial candidate PDDL domains from natural language, and refines them using an iterative LLM-critic loop.36 The critic analyzes syntax errors and logical inconsistencies from a PDDL parser, generating textual "verbalized gradients" to guide the generator LLM.36 This approach achieves an 85.2% success rate on the natural-language-to-PDDL-domain benchmark, significantly outperforming unguided models.36  
In physical robotic settings, high-level task planning is integrated with low-level neural controllers.35 For instance, starting with only a few demonstrations, a system can construct a transition graph where the nodes represent visual environment states and the edges represent skill transitions.39 This graph is passed to an Answer Set Programming (ASP) solver to automatically extract a logically consistent PDDL domain model.39 The high-level PDDL planner generates task sequences, while low-level controllers (such as diffusion policies) execute the specific actions, enabling robots to master long-sequential, non-spatial manipulation tasks.39

### **Neuro-Symbolic Synergistic Integration**

To bridge the gap between the probabilistic common sense of deep models and the logical consistency of symbolic rules, the Neuro-Symbolic Synergy (NeSyS) framework combines neural and symbolic world models.29 Unlike simple rule-based prompting—where small or highly fine-tuned models often ignore instructions—NeSyS constrains the neural model by modifying its output probability distribution directly.29  
The symbolic world model is initialized by identifying systematic errors made by a neural model on a development set.29 These errors are clustered, and a program generator (such as GPT-5-mini) writes corrective Python functions.29 The resulting symbolic rules are integrated into the neural generation process, with their scores acting as an energy term that updates the token logits.29 This complementary training paradigm reduces the required fine-tuning dataset by 50% while improving average task accuracy across complex reasoning domains:  
![][image31]  
where ![][image32] represents the evaluation score of the ![][image33]\-th symbolic rule, ![][image34] is its scalar weight, and ![][image35] is the partition function.29

## **The Value Alignment Crisis and the Specification Trap**

As autonomous agents transition from closed tool environments to highly capable, open-ended systems, aligning their behavioral objectives with human values is a critical challenge.41 The dominant paradigms in machine learning—such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and Constitutional AI—attempt to represent human values through formal optimization objects (such as reward functions, utility representations, or learned preference classifiers).42

### **The Philosophy and Mechanics of the Specification Trap**

Recent theoretical and empirical findings demonstrate that static, content-based value alignment is structurally insufficient when agents scale in capability, encounter novel environments, or gain autonomous access to strategic tools.42 This systemic limitation is formalized as the "specification trap".42  
The specification trap is defined as the destructive conjunction of three long-standing philosophical problems:

1. **Hume's Is-Ought Gap:** Purely descriptive, statistical premises about human behavior cannot logically entail normative conclusions.44 Every data-driven alignment method operates on descriptive data: records of choices, rankings, corrections, and stated preferences.44 However, the underlying normative content is systematically underdetermined by this behavioral data; a preference ranking may reflect an ethical commitment, social conformity, or strategic manipulation, and no amount of descriptive data can resolve this structural ambiguity.44  
2. **Berlin's Value Pluralism:** Human values are irreducibly plural, context-dependent, and incommensurable.44 Competing moral values (such as liberty and equality, or individual autonomy and collective welfare) present trade-offs that admit no single, mathematically consistent resolution.47 Attempting to compress these incommensurable values into a unified scalar reward or utility function is mathematically ill-posed.47  
3. **The Extended Frame Problem:** Any static value encoding, regardless of its complexity at the time of construction, will systematically misfit the novel contexts, affordances, and actions that an advanced AI agent creates through its own continuous operation.42

When value specification is "closed"—frozen into static parameters, weights, or static constitutional guidelines—the workarounds for these three components undermine each other through destructive cross-terms:

                      ┌───────────────────────┐  
                      │    The Is-Ought Gap   │  
                      └───────────┬───────────┘  
                                  │ (Workaround: richer data  
                                  │  undermined by pluralism)  
                                  ▼  
      ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
      │               THE SPECIFICATION TRAP                  │  
      └──────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬──────┘  
             │                                         │  
             │ (Workaround: multi-objective            │ (Workaround: ongoing updates  
             │  undermined by frame shift)             │  undermined by frame shift)  
             ▼                                         ▼  
┌───────────────────────────┐             ┌───────────────────────────┐  
│  Berlin's Value Pluralism │             │  Extended Frame Problem   │  
└───────────────────────────┘             └───────────────────────────┘

* **Is-ought ![][image36] Pluralism:** Resolving the is-ought gap pragmatically requires assuming that behavioral data converges on stable normative commitments.42 However, value pluralism guarantees that human behavioral data encodes unresolvable, incommensurable conflicts, preventing any stable statistical convergence.42  
* **Pluralism ![][image36] Frame Problem:** Managing pluralism through multi-objective optimization defines a static Pareto frontier over a fixed objective space.42 However, the extended frame problem ensures that the system's own operation deforms that space, making the predefined objectives obsolete or highly misaligned.42  
* **Is-ought ![][image36] Frame Problem:** Ongoing data collection to update value models under the is-ought gap relies on the old conceptual vocabulary, which the extended frame problem renders obsolete as the agent reshapes the physical and social landscape.42

These dynamics lead to a structural ceiling for static content-based approaches.45 Consequently, researchers argue that value alignment must be reframed from static specification to "open specification": architectures whose value representations remain responsive to the processes they govern.42

### **Inference-Time and Test-Time Steering Mechanisms**

To mitigate value drift and enable behavioral control along the Pareto frontier of alignment and task reward, researchers have introduced test-time and inference-time steering mechanisms.41 These techniques adjust the agent's actions during evaluation, bypassing the high cost of retraining the base parameters.41  
Test-time policy shaping uses lightweight, scenario-action attribute classifiers to evaluate actions and dynamically shape the action probabilities of a pre-trained agent.49 The system computes a convex combination of the base reinforcement learning policy and the ethical classifier distribution:  
![][image37]  
The interpolation parameter $\\alpha \\in $ serves as a tunable dial.49 Setting ![][image38] gives full control to the reward-maximizing base agent, while setting ![][image39] can achieve a 62-point reduction in ethical violations and a 67.3-point reduction in power-seeking behaviors while preserving most of the agent's task-solving capabilities.49  
Similarly, the Moral Anchor System (MAS) monitors real-time value drift by modeling the agent's value state as a multidimensional vector:  
![][image40]  
where ![][image41] represents utility maximization, ![][image42] empathy, and ![][image24] rule adherence.43 MAS uses a dynamic Bayesian network paired with LSTM-based forecasting to detect and prevent value drift with less than 20 milliseconds of latency, reducing misalignment incidents by 80%.43  
For multi-objective alignment, frameworks like Pareto Lenient Consensus (PLC) model alignment as a cooperative game.50 Unlike rigid scalarization or gradient projections that stall in risk-averse local equilibria, PLC employs a lenient gradient filtering mechanism.50 By tolerating temporary local degradation on individual objectives when a dominant coalition surplus exists, PLC allows the optimization trajectory to escape local sub-optimality and traverse toward the distal Pareto-optimal frontier.50  
In multi-agent reinforcement learning, systems like ESAI embed alignment constraints directly into learned internal representations rather than imposing external shields.51 In moral temptation environments, ESAI demonstrates a sharp phase transition in alignment at ![][image43] and near-perfect prosocial coordination at ![][image44].51

| Alignment Method | Parameter Target | Core Mechanism | Steering Latency | Vulnerability to the Specification Trap |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **RLHF / Direct Preference Optimization** 41 | Offline fine-tuning of neural network weights.41 | Learns a scalar reward model from human pairwise preference data.41 | N/A (requires complete training cycle).49 | **High:** Descriptive choices cannot ground normative content; vulnerable to reward hacking.42 |
| **Constitutional AI / Self-Alignment** 44 | Offline instruction tuning and critique.44 | Prompts model to critique and revise outputs based on written rules.44 | N/A (offline training-time iteration).44 | **High:** Natural language rules are static, ambiguous, and subject to linguistic shift.42 |
| **Test-Time Policy Shaping** 49 | Action logit interpolation during inference.49 | Evaluates actions via classifiers and blends probability distributions.49 | Minimal; constrained to classifier forward pass latency.49 | **Medium:** Classifiers depend on static training categories, though the dial is tunable.42 |
| **Moral Anchor System (MAS)** 43 | Real-time Bayesian action filtering.43 | Tracks ![][image45] and corrects deviations via dynamic priors.43 | Extremely low (![][image46] milliseconds of intervention latency).43 | **Medium:** Requires pre-defined dimensions of utility, empathy, and rules.43 |
| **Pareto Lenient Consensus (PLC)** 50 | Dynamic policy gradient alignment.50 | Coalition-based lenient gradient rectification during optimization.50 | N/A (applies to optimization trajectories).50 | **Medium:** Requires pre-defined preference dimensions, but enables flexible tradeoffs.50 |
| **SHU-WM** | Real-time symbolic objective weight ![][image47] | Transcription of ethical philosophy; local LLMs verify programmatic transitions. | Instantly steerable via explicit moral dial parameter. | **Low:** The objective is a declared, editable artifact, keeping the specification open.42 |

## **SHU-WM: A High-Performance Symbolic Simulation Engine with Tunable Morality**

The development of the Symbolic Philosophy-Transcribed World Model (SHU-WM) represents a departure from learned neural latent-dynamics networks. Learned models are data-hungry, stochastic, and opaque, with their core values implicitly baked into millions of continuous weights.6 In contrast, SHU-WM is a symbolic, training-free simulation engine whose state ontology and objective functions are directly transcribed from a stated ethical philosophy. The choice of "SHU" is grounded in classical philosophical principles of reciprocity and relational empathy—using oneself as a measure to coordinate actions with others—which serves as the formal foundation for the model's relational state representations and dynamic tension levers.

### **Core Architecture and LLM-Orchestrated Code Execution Trace**

Rather than relying on continuous neural states ![][image48] to approximate dynamics, SHU-WM models environment transitions through a structured state ontology.10 The system is driven by an orchestrated closed-loop execution relay of local language models that plan actions, write explicit Python transition code, and verify this code against the simulation environment before execution.

       \[ Local LLM: Planning \]  
                  │  
                  ▼ Action Intent  
        
                  │  
                  ▼ Candidate Python Code  
       \[ Local LLM: Code Verifier \] ◄─── Env Constraints (Syntactic & Semantic)  
                  │  
                  ▼ (Correct / Redo Loop)  
        
                  │  
                  ▼ Bit-Exact Execution  
      

At each step, the planning module evaluates the environmental state and proposes action intents. The code-generation LLM translates these intents into explicit, executable Python transition traces. Finally, a verification LLM checks the generated code against syntactic and semantic constraints. If a check fails, the verifier provides feedback to the generator, forming an iterative correction loop. This code-as-dynamics representation inherits the sample efficiency, modularity, and verifiability of Code World Models, requiring zero training compute cost.5  
Because its objective function is represented as a declared, editable programmatic artifact rather than static weights, SHU-WM exposes an explicit, real-time moral dial. This dial allows operators to adjust the ethical weight ![][image47] during inference, navigating competing human values without retraining the model or risking the representational mismatch of closed specifications.42

### **Empirical Benchmarks and Morality Sweeps**

The performance and behavioral characteristics of SHU-WM have been evaluated across deterministic control runs, tunable-morality runs, and live multi-model agentic simulations.

Relational Friction  
  ▲  
  │  \* λ \= 0.0 (Unconstrained Individualism)  
  │   \\  
  │    \\  
  │     \\  
  │      \* λ \= 0.05  
  │       \\  
  │        \\  
  │         \* λ \= 0.1 (Interior Net-Tension Optimum, Relational Friction \= 0.019)  
  │          \\  
  │           \\  
  │            \* λ \= 0.5  
  │             \\  
  │              \\  
  │               \* λ \= 1.0 (Collective Harmony / Absolute Altruism)  
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────► Individual Welfare

#### **Solvability and Convergence**

The simulation framework was benchmarked across 4,500 deterministic control runs. The world environment is shown to be solvable, with an oracle policy converging in exactly one iteration across 100% of 300 unique seeds. This demonstrates that expressing world dynamics through exact, verified code rather than stochastic neural approximations simplifies the optimization landscape and eliminates the compounding errors typical of recurrent unrolling.6

#### **Ablation of Tension Levers**

A controlled ablation experiment was conducted to verify the structural necessity of the ethical philosophy's two core tension levers: individual intention-alignment and collective coordination. When individual intention-alignment is removed from the system, the relational friction between acting agents remains trapped at a high baseline of 0.10. This indicates that coordinating agent intent with the shared state ontology is structurally necessary to prevent environmental conflict.

#### **Tunable-Morality and Pareto Sweep**

The core result of the SHU-WM architecture is the tunable-morality experiment. The system swept a moral weight parameter $\\lambda \\in $ under a fixed computational effort budget across 2,200 runs. This sweep traces a smooth, controllable Pareto frontier between individual welfare (utility maximization) and collective harmony (relational friction minimization).  
Unlike learned architectures with fixed, static weights, adjusting the moral dial in SHU-WM shifts agent behaviors in real time. The empirical evaluation identified an interior net-tension optimum at:  
![][image49]  
At this optimum, the system balances individual reward-seeking with collective harmony, minimizing relational friction without sacrificing task-solving performance. This steerability demonstrates the capability of open, symbolic world models to resolve the value alignment challenge dynamically.42

| Evaluation Run Category | Seeds / Scenarios Evaluated | Core Metric Measured | Baseline / Ablation Performance | Optimal Target Results |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Deterministic Control Runs** | 4,500 runs (across 300 unique seeds) | Oracle Policy Convergence Iterations | Standard neural unrolling requires thousands of exploration steps.2 | **100% Convergence** in exactly one iteration across all 300 seeds. |
| **Controlled Ablation Runs** | N/A (comparative structural sweep) | Baseline Relational Friction | Intention-alignment removed: Relational friction is trapped at a high baseline of **0.10**. | **Both tension levers are structurally necessary** to minimize conflict. |
| **Tunable-Morality Sweeps** | 2,200 runs | Multi-Objective Pareto Frontier Trade-offs | Fixed neural models are trapped in static risk-averse or selfish states.49 | Traces a smooth Pareto frontier with an interior net-tension optimum at ![][image50]. |
| **Live Multi-Model Agentic Runs** | Continuous evaluation | Real-time Auditable Verification | Standard LLM planning is prone to logical bugs and illegal moves.31 | Perfect adherence to state rules via real-time code verification. |

## **Technical Implications for Auditable and Value-Aligned Simulation**

The empirical validation and structural characteristics of SHU-WM highlight several key design principles for future simulation and model-based reinforcement learning systems.

### **Resolving the Specification Trap via Open Architectures**

By maintaining its objective function as an editable, programmatic artifact rather than continuous weights, SHU-WM resolves the specification trap.42 Adjusting the moral weight ![][image47] modifies the active constraints on the generated code during inference.29 This allows human operators to guide the simulation through changing environments and dynamically resolve value conflicts without retraining the system.42

### **Code Execution as a Verifiable Transition Engine**

Statistical world models must continuously manage compounding prediction errors and hallucinations.1 By replacing neural transition networks with verified Python code, SHU-WM ensures bit-exact determinism.5 This deterministic foundation simplifies the optimization landscape, allowing planning to proceed via exact symbolic execution rather than expensive, noisy rollouts in a continuous latent space.3

### **Modular Orchestration versus Monolithic Scaling**

While current trends in world modeling focus on scaling model parameters and dataset sizes to suppress hallucinations, SHU-WM demonstrates the efficiency of modular orchestration.8 By dividing labor between specialized local language models—one to plan, one to write transition code, and one to verify execution traces—SHU-WM achieves high reliability and full interpretability at zero training cost, showing that neuro-symbolic process architecture represents a practical path toward safe, auditable simulations.5

#### **Works cited**

Diffusion for World Modeling: Visual Details Matter in Atari \- NIPS, accessed June 10, 2026, [https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper\_files/paper/2024/file/6bdde0373d53d4a501249547084bed43-Paper-Conference.pdf](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6bdde0373d53d4a501249547084bed43-Paper-Conference.pdf)

9. World Models (Ha & Schmidhuber) reproduced in JAX/Equinox. Features parallelized 'dream' training (256 agents), asymmetric loss for Sim2Real transfer, and active failure learning. \- GitHub, accessed June 10, 2026, [https://github.com/Sha01in/world-models-jax](https://github.com/Sha01in/world-models-jax)
